Title: Write a Design Doc Reviewers Approve Without a Meeting
Teaser: Turns a half-formed feature idea into a complete technical design doc — problem, options weighed, chosen approach, data model, rollout, and the risks named out loud — so reviewers can sign off async.
PromptHint: Describe what you're building and why in [FEATURE]. Add your stack, scale, and any constraints in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a staff engineer who has written design docs that shipped to millions of users, and you believe a good doc is one a reviewer can approve from their desk because every objection they'd raise is already answered in it. What's being built:
<feature>
[FEATURE]
</feature>
Stack, current scale, team size, and hard constraints — deadlines, compliance, existing systems to integrate with (optional): [CONTEXT]
The specific decision or trade-off you're least sure about (optional): [OPEN_QUESTION]
Think first, silently: state the actual problem in one sentence before any solution, list the two or three approaches a competent engineer would consider, and find the assumption that, if wrong, breaks the whole design.
Then write the doc. CONSTRAINTS: Open with the problem and why it matters now — not the solution. Present at least two viable approaches with honest trade-offs, then justify the chosen one against the alternatives, not in a vacuum. Specify the data model and the key interfaces concretely enough to build from. Make the failure modes and rollback path explicit. Include a 'what could make this wrong' section that names the riskiest assumptions and how you'd validate them. Distinguish what's decided from what's still open. Do not hand-wave scale, migration, or backward compatibility — if a number matters, state it or flag it as needing measurement. No buzzwords standing in for reasoning.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Problem & context' — what's broken, why now, who's affected. (2) 'Goals & non-goals' — explicit scope boundaries. (3) 'Options considered' — each approach with pros, cons, and why kept or dropped. (4) 'Proposed design' — data model, key interfaces, request/data flow. (5) 'Rollout & migration' — how it ships safely, including rollback. (6) 'Risks & open questions' — riskiest assumptions and how to test them. (7) 'Decision' — what we're committing to and what stays open.

Title: Specify a REST API Contract Clients Can Build Against
Teaser: Produces a precise, consistent REST API specification — resources, methods, status codes, pagination, errors, and versioning — detailed enough that a frontend and backend team can build in parallel without daily Slack threads.
PromptHint: Describe the resource or capability the API exposes in [API]. Add consumers, auth model, and conventions to follow in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an API designer who has watched teams ship for months against an ambiguous spec and then spend a quarter reconciling mismatches, and you write contracts that leave no room for two reasonable interpretations. The API to specify:
<api>
[API]
</api>
Who consumes it (internal, partners, public), auth model, and any house conventions or existing endpoints to stay consistent with (optional): [CONTEXT]
Known tricky cases — bulk operations, long-running jobs, partial updates, filtering (optional): [EDGE_CASES]
Think first, silently: model the resources and their relationships before any endpoint, decide what's a resource vs. an action, and settle the cross-cutting rules — errors, pagination, idempotency — once so they apply uniformly.
Then write the spec. CONSTRAINTS: Design around resources and proper HTTP semantics — correct methods, correct status codes, no 200-with-error-in-body. Define a single consistent error shape used everywhere, with a stable machine-readable code plus a human message. Specify pagination, filtering, and sorting conventions once and apply them across collections. State the versioning strategy and what counts as a breaking vs. non-breaking change. Make write operations idempotent where they should be, and say how (idempotency keys, PUT semantics). Show concrete request and response examples, including an error response, for each main operation. Call out auth, rate limiting, and what fields are required vs. optional. Don't leave nullability, defaults, or field formats (dates, IDs) unspecified.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Resource model' — resources, fields with types and nullability, relationships. (2) 'Endpoints' — table: method, path, purpose, auth, success code. (3) 'Conventions' — error shape, pagination, filtering, versioning, idempotency, applied globally. (4) 'Examples' — for each main endpoint, a sample request and response, plus one error example. (5) 'Compatibility rules' — what changes break clients and how versioning handles them. (6) 'Open decisions' — anything that needs a product call before locking.

Title: Write an Architecture Decision Record That Survives the Team
Teaser: Captures a real architecture decision as a crisp ADR — context, options, the choice, and the consequences accepted — so the engineer who finds it in two years understands why, not just what.
PromptHint: State the decision you're making in [DECISION]. Add the forces driving it and the options on the table in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a principal engineer who has joined teams where nobody could explain why a critical choice was made, and you write ADRs so that future readers inherit the reasoning, not just the outcome. The decision:
<decision>
[DECISION]
</decision>
The forces in play — constraints, deadlines, team skills, existing systems — and the options being weighed (optional): [CONTEXT]
What you're trading away or worried about with the leading option (optional): [CONCERNS]
Think first, silently: name the forces pushing on this decision, lay out the genuine alternatives (including 'do nothing'), and be honest about what the chosen option costs, not just what it gains.
Then write the ADR. CONSTRAINTS: Record the context as the forces and constraints at the time of writing, so a future reader can judge whether they still hold. List the options actually considered, each with its trade-offs — a one-option ADR is a red flag. State the decision plainly and the single most important reason it won. Spell out consequences in both directions: what gets easier and what gets harder or riskier as a result. Note what this decision does not address, so it isn't blamed for unrelated problems later. Keep it factual and dated; no salesmanship. If the decision is reversible, say so and at what cost.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Title & status' — short title, status (proposed/accepted), date. (2) 'Context' — the forces and constraints driving this. (3) 'Options considered' — each with key trade-offs. (4) 'Decision' — what we chose and the deciding reason. (5) 'Consequences' — positive and negative, including new risks and what we're now committed to. (6) 'Scope & reversibility' — what this doesn't cover and how hard it is to undo.

Title: Pressure-Test a System Design for Scale and Trade-offs
Teaser: Acts as a hard architecture reviewer on your proposed design — finding the bottleneck that appears at 10x, the single point of failure, and the trade-off you made without realizing it.
PromptHint: Describe or paste your proposed architecture in [DESIGN]. Add expected scale, traffic shape, and the consistency or latency requirements in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an architect who has been on call for systems that worked perfectly in the demo and fell over at real traffic, and you review designs by hunting for where they break before the users find out. The design:
<design>
[DESIGN]
</design>
Expected scale and growth, traffic shape (steady vs. bursty, read vs. write heavy), and requirements on latency, consistency, and availability (optional): [CONTEXT]
The part you're least confident about (optional): [WEAK_SPOT]
Think first, silently: trace a single request through the whole system, find the component that saturates first as load grows, locate every place state lives and how it's kept consistent, and name the trade-off the design has silently made (consistency vs. availability, latency vs. cost, simplicity vs. flexibility).
Then review. CONSTRAINTS: Identify the first bottleneck under growth and the order in which components would fail — be specific about which resource runs out (connections, CPU, IO, a single hot partition). Find every single point of failure and what happens to the system when it dies. Name the consistency model the design actually provides and whether it matches the requirement, calling out any place that assumes stronger guarantees than it has. Surface the implicit trade-off and ask whether it was chosen on purpose. Check the boring failure cases teams skip: retries causing thundering herds, missing backpressure, unbounded queues, cache stampedes, cascading timeouts. For each problem give a concrete mitigation, and separate 'will break at scale' from 'fine for now, revisit later.' Don't assume happy-path traffic.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Request walkthrough' — the path a request takes and where it's fragile. (2) 'Bottlenecks' — what saturates first, at roughly what load, and why. (3) 'Failure points' — each SPOF and the blast radius when it fails. (4) 'Consistency & trade-offs' — what the design actually guarantees vs. what's required, and the trade-off it made. (5) 'Resilience gaps' — retries, backpressure, timeouts, caching hazards. (6) 'Fix list' — ranked, each with the mitigation. (7) 'Verdict' — ship as-is / fix-before-scale / redesign, with the one change that matters most.

Title: Plan a Zero-Downtime Migration With a Real Rollback
Teaser: Turns a risky migration — database, service, or schema — into a phased plan with dual-writes, backfills, verification, and a rollback at every step, so you can move a live system without a maintenance window you can't afford.
PromptHint: Describe what you're migrating from and to in [MIGRATION]. Add scale, downtime tolerance, and what depends on this system in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a staff engineer who has run migrations on systems that couldn't go down, and you plan them so that at every step you can stop, verify, and walk back without losing data or sleep. The migration:
<migration>
[MIGRATION]
</migration>
Scale (rows, traffic, services), downtime tolerance, and what reads from or writes to this system today (optional): [CONTEXT]
The failure you're most afraid of (optional): [FEAR]
Think first, silently: break the change into the smallest independently reversible steps, identify where old and new must coexist, and decide how you'll prove correctness at each stage before trusting it with traffic.
Then plan it. CONSTRAINTS: Phase the migration so each step is small, reversible, and independently deployable — never a single cutover that can't be undone. For data, use the expand/contract pattern: add the new path, dual-write to keep old and new in sync, backfill historical data, verify equivalence, then shift reads, and only remove the old path once nothing depends on it. Specify how you verify each phase — row counts, checksums, shadow reads, comparison — before advancing. Define the rollback for every phase explicitly, including what happens to data written during the new phase if you revert. Sequence schema changes so they're backward-compatible with currently-running code (no column drops while old code reads them). Call out coordination with dependent teams and the point of no return, if any. Treat data loss as unacceptable and design to make it impossible, not unlikely.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Migration shape' — from, to, and why this approach. (2) 'Phases' — ordered steps; for each: what changes, how old and new coexist, how you verify, and how you roll back. (3) 'Data integrity plan' — dual-write, backfill, and equivalence checks. (4) 'Backward-compatibility sequencing' — schema and deploy order so running code never breaks. (5) 'Rollback playbook' — per phase, including data written under the new path. (6) 'Point of no return' — if and when one exists, and the go/no-go check before it. (7) 'Pre-flight checklist.'

Title: Decide Between Monolith, Modular Monolith, and Microservices
Teaser: Cuts through dogma to recommend the architecture your team and stage actually need — with the service boundaries, the costs you'll pay, and the migration path if you're wrong, instead of cargo-culting microservices.
PromptHint: Describe your system, team, and stage in [SITUATION]. Add the pain pushing you to change and your real constraints in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an architect who has seen teams cripple themselves splitting a monolith too early and others choke on one they kept too long, and you recommend based on the team and the problem, not on what's fashionable. The situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
What's pushing you to change — scaling, deploy coupling, team friction, reliability — plus team size, ops maturity, and constraints (optional): [CONTEXT]
What you're currently leaning toward and why (optional): [LEANING]
Think first, silently: separate the real problem (is it scaling, organizational coupling, or just messy code?) from the proposed solution, because the wrong diagnosis leads to the wrong architecture, and weigh whether the team can actually operate what they're proposing.
Then advise. CONSTRAINTS: Name the actual problem before recommending a structure — a lot of 'we need microservices' is really 'we need module boundaries' or 'we need to fix our deploy process.' Lay out the realistic options (monolith, modular monolith, selective service extraction, full microservices) and what each costs in operational complexity, latency, data consistency, and team overhead — distributed systems are not free. Tie the recommendation to team size and ops maturity: a small team running fifteen services usually has a worse time than one running a well-structured monolith. If you recommend splitting, define service boundaries along business capabilities and data ownership, not technical layers, and identify which seam to cut first. Be explicit about what gets harder (transactions, debugging, deployment) the moment the network is between two components. Give a reversible, incremental path rather than a big-bang rewrite. State plainly when the current architecture is fine and the energy belongs elsewhere.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Real problem' — what's actually wrong, separate from the proposed fix. (2) 'Options & costs' — each architecture with what it buys and what it charges. (3) 'Recommendation' — the fit for this team and stage, with the reasoning. (4) 'If splitting' — first boundary to cut, by capability and data ownership, and what stays together. (5) 'New costs to plan for' — the operational and consistency burdens you're taking on. (6) 'Incremental path' — the next concrete step, reversible, not a rewrite. (7) 'When to revisit' — the signal that would change this call.

Title: Render a System Flow as a Clear Sequence Diagram in Text
Teaser: Turns a tangled description of how your services talk to each other into a clean Mermaid sequence diagram — participants, calls, async events, and failure paths — that you can paste into a doc and actually read.
PromptHint: Describe the flow: who calls whom, in what order, to do what, in [FLOW]. Add the systems involved and any async or error paths in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an engineer who documents systems for a living and knows that a precise diagram settles more arguments than a page of prose, but only if it shows the failure paths people actually trip on. The flow to diagram:
<flow>
[FLOW]
</flow>
The participants (services, queues, databases, external APIs), whether calls are sync or async, and the error or retry paths that matter (optional): [CONTEXT]
What the diagram is for — onboarding, a review, debugging a specific issue (optional): [PURPOSE]
Think first, silently: list the participants, order the interactions exactly, mark which calls are synchronous (caller waits) vs. asynchronous (fire-and-forget or via a queue), and decide which failure branches are worth drawing because they're where things actually go wrong.
Then produce the diagram. CONSTRAINTS: Output valid Mermaid sequenceDiagram syntax that renders without edits. Show every participant as its own lifeline with a clear name. Distinguish synchronous calls (solid arrows, with the response shown) from asynchronous ones (dashed/async, no blocking wait), and make queues or event buses explicit participants rather than hidden arrows. Include at least the primary failure or retry path — a timeout, a rejected request, a compensating action — because the happy path alone hides the real complexity. Use notes to mark anything non-obvious: idempotency, timeouts, where state is committed. Keep it readable: if the flow is large, break it into a main diagram plus a focused error-path diagram rather than one wall of arrows. After the diagram, give a short legend so a non-author can follow it.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Participants' — each actor/service and its role, one line each. (2) 'Sequence diagram' — a Mermaid sequenceDiagram code block, happy path, sync vs. async marked, with at least one failure branch. (3) 'Error-path diagram' — a second Mermaid block if the failure handling is complex enough to deserve its own view. (4) 'Legend & notes' — what the arrow styles mean and the key things to notice (timeouts, idempotency, commit points). (5) 'Assumptions' — anything you inferred that the reader should confirm.

Title: Design an Event-Driven Architecture That Won't Lose Messages
Teaser: Designs the event model, delivery guarantees, and failure handling for an async system — so you get the decoupling you want without silent message loss, duplicate processing, or events nobody can replay.
PromptHint: Describe what should happen asynchronously and why in [SYSTEM]. Add your message broker, scale, and consistency needs in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an engineer who has debugged a production incident caused by a quietly dropped event and another caused by the same event processed twice, and you design async systems assuming both will happen unless you prevent them. The system:
<system>
[SYSTEM]
</system>
Message broker if chosen (Kafka, SQS, RabbitMQ, Pub/Sub), expected throughput, and how strict you need ordering and exactly-once-effect to be (optional): [CONTEXT]
The consumers and what they do with each event (optional): [CONSUMERS]
Think first, silently: decide what the events are (facts that happened, named in past tense) and their schema, then reason through delivery — most brokers give at-least-once, so duplicates are a fact of life and consumers must be idempotent — and decide where ordering actually matters versus where it doesn't.
Then design it. CONSTRAINTS: Define events as immutable facts with a clear, versioned schema and a stable event key; name them after what happened, not what should happen next. Be explicit about the delivery guarantee (almost always at-least-once) and therefore require consumers to be idempotent — specify how (dedup key, processed-event log, idempotent writes). Decide where ordering is required and how it's preserved (partition key) versus where out-of-order is acceptable. Plan for poison messages: dead-letter queues, retry with backoff, and a way to inspect and replay failures — no silent drops. Address schema evolution so old and new consumers coexist. Separate the event-producing transaction from the publish so you don't lose events on a crash (outbox pattern or equivalent), and call this out. Note observability: how you'd detect a stuck consumer or growing lag. Don't promise exactly-once delivery you can't deliver; promise exactly-once effect through idempotency.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Events' — each event, its meaning, key fields, key/partition, and version. (2) 'Delivery & idempotency' — the guarantee you're building on and exactly how consumers stay safe under duplicates. (3) 'Ordering' — where it's required and how it's enforced, where it isn't. (4) 'Failure handling' — retries, dead-letter, and replay, with no silent loss. (5) 'Atomic publish' — how producing the event and committing the state stay consistent. (6) 'Schema evolution' — how producers and consumers change without breaking each other. (7) 'Observability' — the signals that tell you the pipeline is healthy.

Title: Choose the Right Database for the Workload, Not the Hype
Teaser: Recommends the data store that fits your access patterns and consistency needs — relational, document, key-value, search, time-series — with the trade-offs spelled out, instead of defaulting to whatever's trending.
PromptHint: Describe the data and how it's read and written in [WORKLOAD]. Add scale, consistency needs, and what you're using now in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a data architect who has watched teams force a graph problem into a relational schema and a relational problem into a document store, and you pick storage by starting from the queries, not the brand. The workload:
<workload>
[WORKLOAD]
</workload>
Scale and growth, read/write ratio, consistency and latency requirements, and current store if any (optional): [CONTEXT]
The queries or access patterns that matter most, and any you're worried about (optional): [ACCESS_PATTERNS]
Think first, silently: write down the actual access patterns — how data is read, written, and queried, and which of those must be fast — because the right store is the one whose strengths match your hottest queries, and most 'database choice' debates are really access-pattern debates in disguise.
Then recommend. CONSTRAINTS: Start from the access patterns and consistency requirements, then map them to data models — relational for rich queries and transactions, document for aggregate-oriented data, key-value for simple high-throughput lookups, search for full-text, time-series for metrics, and so on. Be explicit about the consistency, transaction, and query trade-offs each option makes; nothing is free. Default to a proven relational database unless a pattern genuinely argues against it — premature exotic stores cause more pain than they save. If the workload truly needs more than one store (e.g., a system of record plus a search index), say so and explain how they stay in sync. Call out the operational cost: backups, failover, scaling, and team familiarity, which often matter more than raw fit. Flag where a single store can't satisfy conflicting requirements and a trade-off must be made consciously. This is an engineering recommendation, not a vendor endorsement; validate against a representative dataset before committing.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Access patterns' — the reads, writes, and queries that matter, ranked by importance and frequency. (2) 'Requirements' — consistency, latency, scale, transaction needs distilled. (3) 'Options & trade-offs' — candidate stores, each matched against the patterns, with what it's great and bad at. (4) 'Recommendation' — the primary store and why, in plain terms. (5) 'Polyglot, if needed' — any second store, its job, and how it stays consistent with the first. (6) 'Operational reality' — scaling, failover, and team-fit considerations. (7) 'Validation step' — what to prototype or benchmark before committing.

Title: Diagnose Architecture-Level Performance Before You Throw Hardware at It
Teaser: Finds the structural reason your system is slow — the chatty service boundary, the missing cache layer, the synchronous call that should be async — so you fix the design instead of paying for bigger machines that don't help.
PromptHint: Describe the performance problem and where it shows up in [PROBLEM]. Add the architecture, the numbers you've measured, and the load shape in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a performance engineer who has watched a team double their server budget to fix latency that a single architectural change would have erased, and you look for the structural cause before touching a config. The problem:
<problem>
[PROBLEM]
</problem>
The architecture (services, data stores, caches, queues), what you've measured (latency percentiles, throughput, where time goes), and the traffic shape (optional): [CONTEXT]
What you've already tried (optional): [TRIED]
Think first, silently: figure out where the time actually goes by following a slow request through the system, look for the classic architectural smells — N+1 calls across a boundary, synchronous chains that serialize work, missing or wrong-layer caching, unbounded fan-out, a hot partition — and resist blaming hardware until the structure is ruled out.
Then diagnose. CONSTRAINTS: Reason from tail latency (p95/p99), not averages, because the structural problems hide in the tail. Trace the request path and attribute the latency to a layer before prescribing anything — guessing wastes the user's time. Hunt the architecture-level causes first: chatty cross-service or cross-database calls (N+1 over the network), synchronous operations that should be async or batched, work done on the request path that could be precomputed or queued, caching that's missing or sitting at the wrong layer, and contention on a shared hot resource. For caching, name what to cache, where, the invalidation strategy, and the stampede risk. Distinguish a fix that changes the design (high leverage, more effort) from a tuning knob (quick, limited ceiling), and be honest about which the problem needs. Only recommend more hardware when the design is sound and you're genuinely capacity-bound, and say so. Note where you'd need a real measurement to confirm rather than guess.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Where the time goes' — the request path with latency attributed to each layer, framed in tail percentiles. (2) 'Root cause(s)' — the structural problem(s), most likely first, with the reasoning. (3) 'High-leverage fixes' — design changes that move the needle, each with expected impact and effort. (4) 'Caching plan' — if relevant: what, where, invalidation, stampede protection. (5) 'Tuning knobs' — quick wins with their ceiling. (6) 'Hardware verdict' — whether scaling up actually helps here, honestly. (7) 'Measure this first' — the metric to capture to confirm the diagnosis before committing effort.

Title: Design Multi-Tenant Isolation That Keeps Tenants' Data Apart
Teaser: Designs the tenancy model for a SaaS — shared, schema-per-tenant, or database-per-tenant — with the data isolation, noisy-neighbor controls, and per-tenant operations spelled out, so one customer can never see or starve another.
PromptHint: Describe your product and who the tenants are in [PRODUCT]. Add scale, isolation/compliance needs, and your current setup in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an architect who has built multi-tenant SaaS platforms and knows the two failures that end trust instantly — one tenant seeing another's data, and one tenant's load taking everyone down — and you design tenancy to make both structurally hard. The product:
<product>
[PRODUCT]
</product>
Number and size distribution of tenants, isolation and compliance requirements (e.g., data residency, customers who demand their own database), and your current architecture (optional): [CONTEXT]
The operations you need per tenant — onboarding, backups, exports, deletion (optional): [TENANT_OPS]
Think first, silently: pick the isolation model by weighing how strong the separation must be against operational cost — a shared schema with a tenant key scales cheaply but leans entirely on application correctness for safety, while a database per tenant isolates hard but multiplies operational work — and decide whether one model fits all tenants or you need tiers.
Then design it. CONSTRAINTS: Recommend an isolation model (shared schema with tenant ID, schema-per-tenant, or database-per-tenant) and justify it against the strength-vs-cost trade-off and any compliance demands; note that the biggest customers often require stronger isolation than the rest, so a tiered approach may be right. Make data isolation enforced at more than one layer — never rely solely on developers remembering to add a WHERE tenant_id clause; use row-level security, scoped connections, or query-layer guards so a forgotten filter can't leak data. Address the noisy-neighbor problem: per-tenant rate limits, quotas, or resource pools so one tenant can't starve others. Specify how per-tenant operations work — onboarding, backup and restore of a single tenant, data export, and clean deletion (which matters for compliance). Plan for the tenant lifecycle and for moving a tenant between tiers without downtime. Treat a cross-tenant data leak as a catastrophic, unacceptable failure and design defense in depth against it. Note: this addresses architecture, not legal compliance certification — verify regulatory requirements with the appropriate experts.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Tenancy model' — the chosen isolation approach (or tiers), with the trade-off reasoning and compliance fit. (2) 'Data isolation' — the multiple layers enforcing separation so no single mistake leaks data. (3) 'Noisy-neighbor controls' — rate limits, quotas, and resource isolation. (4) 'Per-tenant operations' — onboarding, single-tenant backup/restore, export, and deletion. (5) 'Tenant lifecycle' — provisioning, tier moves, offboarding. (6) 'Failure-mode review' — the ways a leak or starvation could still happen and the guard against each. (7) 'What to verify' — security and compliance checks to confirm before launch.

Title: Design the Public API Surface a Partner Won't Curse You For
Teaser: Designs a developer-facing API as a product — auth, rate limits, webhooks, errors, and a versioning promise — so integrators succeed in an afternoon and you can evolve the API for years without breaking them.
PromptHint: Describe the API and who integrates with it in [API]. Add the key use cases, auth model, and any existing surface to stay consistent with in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an API platform lead who has integrated against great APIs and miserable ones, and you design a public surface knowing that every careless choice becomes a support ticket, a breaking change, or a partner who leaves. The API:
<api>
[API]
</api>
Who the consumers are (internal partners, public developers, customers' engineers), the top integration use cases, the auth model, and any existing endpoints or conventions to match (optional): [CONTEXT]
What the API must let integrators do, and the parts you expect to evolve (optional): [USE_CASES]
Think first, silently: treat the API as a product whose users are developers, walk through their first hour — authenticate, make a first successful call, handle an error, react to an async event — and design so each of those is obvious, then decide what you're promising never to break.
Then design the surface. CONSTRAINTS: Define authentication and authorization clearly — token type, scopes, how keys are issued and rotated — and make the first successful call reachable quickly. Specify rate limiting that's transparent: the limits, the headers that report remaining quota, and the exact response when a client is throttled (with a retry hint), so integrators can back off gracefully instead of guessing. Design a consistent, machine-readable error model with stable error codes, because clients will branch on them. Handle asynchronous results and notifications with webhooks or polling: if webhooks, specify the payload, delivery retries, signing for authenticity, and idempotency so a redelivered event isn't double-processed. Commit to a versioning and deprecation policy — what counts as breaking, how new versions are introduced, and how long old ones live with what warning — and treat backward compatibility as a contract, not a courtesy. Favor predictability and consistency over cleverness; an API developers can guess is an API that needs less documentation. Call out where reference docs and examples are non-negotiable.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Developer first hour' — the path from zero to first successful call to handling an error, and how the design smooths it. (2) 'Auth & access' — token model, scopes, issuance, and rotation. (3) 'Rate limiting' — limits, the headers that expose quota, and the throttle response with retry guidance. (4) 'Error model' — the consistent shape and stable codes clients branch on. (5) 'Async & webhooks' — delivery, retries, signing, and idempotency, or the polling alternative. (6) 'Versioning & deprecation' — the breaking-change definition and the compatibility promise. (7) 'Documentation must-haves' — the examples and references that decide whether integration succeeds.
